title: You play, Russia watches: Moscow is using online gaming to recruit “disposable agents”
content:
So-called “disposable agents”, recruited by Russian intelligence services to carry out acts of sabotage such as vandalism and arson, are being widely used. The search for such individuals is also taking place within online video gaming environment.

This spring, journalists from the fact-checking team of the Polish national news agency Polska Agencja Prasowa (PAP) highlighted the issue of disinformation and agent recruitment within gaming environments in a series of reports.

The study noted that online gaming platforms popular among children and young people, such as Roblox and Minecraft, have already been used as recruitment tools by Islamists. Meanwhile, a Russian-made war game has appeared on the popular gaming platform Steam, allowing players to experience the invasion of Ukraine from the occupiers’ perspective.

At the same time, PAP reported that the insufficiently monitored gaming environment has become a recruitment tool for Russian intelligence services. Latvia’s State Security Service (VDD) also confirmed to Delfi that Russian special services are increasingly using video gaming environments for recruitment purposes.

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Do a “very large proportion of asylum seekers” lie about their age? Of those checked, all had liedasylum seekersDo a “very large proportion of asylum seekers” lie about their age? Of those checked, all had lied
content:
Jānis Dombrava is a former Saeima deputy, a board member of the National Alliance “All for Latvia! — For Fatherland and Freedom/LNNK” and deputy chair of the party, who has just become interior minister in the government of Andris Kulbergs (AS).

Delfi asked the Ministry of the Interior (MoI) and the Office of Citizenship and Migration Affairs (OCMA) whether these institutions have data on the age asylum seekers declare themselves and the age established during checks, and whether those data confirm Dombrava’s statement.

The State Border Guard (SBG) “does not compile statistical data on how many persons have changed their personal data during procedural actions, namely initially lied by stating that they are minors,” says the MoI’s reply, prepared together with the OCMA and the SBG. When the SBG begins the initial procedures with an asylum seeker, it informs the person about the asylum procedure and about their rights and obligations during that procedure.

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The Ukrainian, Georgian or European Scenario? Why the EU is suddenly eager to befriend Armenia
content:
The summit included bilateral meetings between Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan and EU politicians, including representatives from Latvia, as well as public speeches by Western leaders full of glowing praise for Armenia’s chosen European path.

There was also no shortage of champagne toasts and the signing of a new EU-Armenia co-operation agreement. But why now? For many years, Armenia was regarded as one of Russia’s closest allies in the Caucasus region. The answer lies in several wars and a revolution.

Nagorno-Karabakh was historically a territory in neighbouring Azerbaijan populated predominantly by ethnic Armenians. The first armed clashes between Armenians and Azerbaijanis began even before the collapse of the Soviet Union and escalated into a full-scale war between the two countries in the early 1990s.
snippet: The summit included bilateral meetings between Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan and EU politicians, including representatives from Latvia, as well as public speeches by Western leaders full of
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Russia’s Shadow Fleet and the Limits of Western Sanctions
content:
In December 2024, an eighteen-year-old tanker dragged its anchor across the floor of the Baltic Sea, severing the power cable linking Estonia and Finland. For several hours, nobody noticed. By the time the source of the failure was identified, the EstLink 2 subsea power cable had been cut, four telecommunications cables were disabled, and the damage amounted to sixty million euros.

The captain of the tanker Eagle S was charged with sabotage after the vessel was accused of causing the destruction. During the inspection, authorities discovered equipment used to monitor NATO naval activity on board. The ship was one of more than seven hundred vessels forming what analysts have begun to describe as Russia’s shadow fleet — a phenomenon unprecedented in scale, organisation and geopolitical consequences.
snippet: In December 2024, an eighteen-year-old tanker dragged its anchor across the floor of the Baltic Sea, severing the power cable linking Estonia and Finland. For several hours, nobody noticed. By the

title: Does the Ministry of Defence of Latvia stand out for critically high staff turnover?
content:
Following the resignation of Defence Minister Andris Sprūds (“Progressives”), accusations have circulated both on social media platforms and in politicians’ statements that the Ministry of Defence (MoD) is experiencing very high staff turnover.

The governing party “New Unity” claims that it has reached as much as 40%. It should be noted that high staff turnover across the state administration as a whole is not new, but “New Unity’s” claims are greatly exaggerated – compared to the background of other ministries, the MoD does not stand out with such dramatic figures.

Claims have circulated widely on the social media platform “X” that “one in six employees at Sprūds’ Ministry of Defence left within a year”. Political opponents were quick to seize on the narrative, amplifying it with remarks such as: “It is a miracle that the institution is still functioning at all.”

Staff turnover at the MoD has also drawn the attention of the governing party “New Unity”, which on Monday published a claim that it had reached as much as 40%. The party’s Facebook account also claimed that, following the latest security incidents, there had been a “loss of public trust”.
snippet: Following the resignation of Defence Minister Andris Sprūds (“Progressives”), accusations have circulated both on social media platforms and in politicians’ statements that the Ministry of Defence
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: NATO 3.0: Europe takes the lead in its own defence
content:
Europe has embarked on a gradual path toward greater defense independence from the United States. At the center of this transformation is a redefinition of NATO’s internal balance—one that could reshape the alliance’s structure, burden-sharing model, and even the long-term role of American forces in Europe. 

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte frames this evolution as a “trajectory” toward what some analysts are calling NATO 3.0—a model built on a stronger European pillar and reduced reliance on Washington as the alliance’s primary security guarantor.

“NATO is – and will always be – a transatlantic Alliance. Part of keeping this Alliance strong involves shifting responsibilities. Moving away from unhealthy over-reliance on one Ally to a fairer sharing of the responsibility for our collective security,” Rutte said before last week’s meeting of NATO foreign ministers in Helsingborg, Sweden.

From NATO 1.0 to NATO 3.0

The

title: Hybrid threaths on Eastern Flank (INFOGRAPHIC)
content:
There are growing calls for the EU mutual defence clause (Article 42.7 TEU) to play a significant role in ensuring the security of EU member states, particularly in the event of hybrid threats and cyberattacks.

The threat is real: the infographic presents selected incidents related to hybrid threats that have occurred on the eastern flank in recent years.

The post Hybrid threaths on Eastern Flank (INFOGRAPHIC) appeared first on Eastflank.

creator: Zofia Grosse

title: EU and NATO defence clauses should complement each other, Eastern Flank states think
content:
Although countries on the EU’s eastern flank are just beginning to discuss the use of the EU’s defence clause, they recognize that it could be particularly useful in the event of hybrid and cyberattacks. However, they continue to rely primarily on NATO for their security.

When EU leaders gathered for their informal summit in Cyprus in April, Article 42.7 of the Treaty on European Union was on the table for the first time. The clause obliges every EU member state to provide “aid and assistance by all means in their power” if another is the victim of armed aggression.

It has been invoked exactly once – in November 2015, when France activated the clause after the terrorist attacks in Paris. Even then, the mechanism produced no coordinated EU response.

In Nicosia, European Council President António Costa acknowledged publicly that the discussion was now about how the clause “can be used in practice.” The EU’s High Representative briefed leaders on ongoing efforts, including the early-stage drafting of a handbook.

The next step was a so-called table read at the Political and Security Committee (PSC) i
snippet: Although countries on the EU’s eastern flank are just beginning to discuss the use of the EU’s defence clause, they recognize that it could be particularly useful in the event of hybrid and cyberattac
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Did the EU declare war on emojis? We checked the claim spread by Slovak far-right politicians and Polymarket
content:
Brussels recently warned that some emojis are being used as coded references for illegal activities such as child pornography or drug use. Some accounts, however, misrepresented the warning as a call to ban these emojis altogether.

Since the Digital Services Act (DSA) came fully into force across the EU in 2024, plenty has been written about it. 

Its main goal is to set rules for large online platforms and make the internet a safer and more transparent space. Even so, its provisions are often misinterpreted and trigger significant backlash.

Most recently, claims began circulating that the EU wants to restrict – or even ban – some commonly used emojis. In recent weeks, the narrative was spread by the American platform Polymarket, among others.

“JUST IN: EU to push for crackdown on emojis that can be used to cover up “illegal speech,” the betting platform
snippet: Brussels recently warned that some emojis are being used as coded references for illegal activities such as child pornography or drug use. Some accounts, however, misrepresented the warning as a call
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Inventing Chaos: The Fake Narratives Around Max Korzh’s Bucharest Concert
content:
The concert of Belarusian rapper Max Korzh, which gathered tens of thousands of people at Arena Națională in Bucharest – especially young people – was both a demonstration of freedom and a trigger for online hatred.

Max Korzh, who performs in Russian, held a sold-out concert in Bucharest on May 23, attended by more than 42,000 spectators – most of them coming from outside Romania, including Belarus, Ukraine, the Republic of Moldova, Poland, and other Eastern European countries. Korzh is a Belarusian citizen living in exile and is banned from Russia, Belarus, and Ukraine alike – the latter because he performed in Crimea after Moscow’s illegal annexation of the peninsula.

A few days before the concert, Romanian authorities announced an expanded security operation due to the large number of attendees and the international profile of the audience. Gendarmes, police officers, and public order units carried out pyrotechnic screenings and anti-terrorism checks, while service dogs and mounted police were also deployed. Compared to other concerts of similar or even larger scale, Korzh’s arrival in Bucharest led to an unusually large m

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: EU faces “worst energy crisis in history,” Commissioner says
content:
The European Union is confronting its most severe energy crisis on record, yet the bloc is now significantly better prepared than during the shock of 2022, EU Commissioner for Energy and Housing Dan Jørgensen told FocusEurope.pl.

The remarks come amid escalating geopolitical tensions and volatile energy markets. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively blocked, while Brent crude has surged to record levels in recent days. On May 6, prices briefly climbed above $114 per barrel — the highest since the onset of the U.S.-Israeli operation in Iran.

“We are facing the worst energy crisis in history. Many say the current crisis is worse than the crises of 1973 and 2022 combined,” Jørgensen told FocusEurope.pl and other media in Brussels.

He estimated that the EU is currently spending around €500 million per day to manage the economic fallout. “That is a clear sign of Europe’s structural weakness,” he added.

Better prepared than in 2022

Despite the severity of the crisis, Jørgensen argued the EU’s resilience has improved markedly since 2022. He poin
snippet: The European Union is confronting its most severe energy crisis on record, yet the bloc is now significantly better prepared than during the shock of 2022, EU Commissioner for Energy and Housing Dan J
creator: Aleksandra Krzysztoszek

title: From “Progressive Bulgaria” to “Bangaranga” – Between Two Victories and a New European Narrative
content:
Within less than a month, Bulgaria experienced two seemingly unrelated events that gradually began to complement each other and mutually reinforce one another.

Two individuals, two striking victories – one in a music competition in Europe, the other in the struggle for political power in Bulgaria.

If there is one word for the month of May, it is “Bangaranga.” The catchy song by the young singer Dara, with which she achieved an impressive victory for Bulgaria at Eurovision, sparked euphoria in the country and a strong resonance across Europe.

Her victory inevitably became part of European politics amid tensions between the European Union and Israel, whose representative finished second. And by the largest margin between first and second place in the entire 70-year history of Eurovision.

In Bulgaria, the effect was even stronger. As the winner, the country automatically becomes the host of the next edition of the contest. In other circumstances, this would have been mainly an organizational and logistical matter, but the decisive victory of Rumen Radev and his party “Progressive Bulgaria” gave th
snippet: Within less than a month, Bulgaria experienced two seemingly unrelated events that gradually began to complement each other and mutually reinforce one another. Two individuals, two striking victories
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: ICE deportations through Poland: Were Ukrainians sent from the US straight to the front?
content:
Amnesty International and Human Rights First have raised concerns that Poland may have been involved in the deportation of Ukrainian citizens from the United States to a country at war. 

The organisations claim that some of those deported were later sent to the front line after crossing into Ukraine. Warsaw rejects the accusations, insisting that it merely acted as a transit state.

On Wednesday, 18 November 2025, a deportation flight operated by the US Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency (ICE) landed at Rzeszów-Jasionka airport in south-eastern Poland. On board were 50 Ukrainian nationals deported from the United States.

No relatives or friends were waiting for them. They were not transferred to a refugee reception centre or even a regular hotel. Instead, they were transported directly from the airport to the Medyka–Shehyni border crossing and handed over to the Ukrainian authorities. According to a CNN investigation, some of them were later deployed to the front line.

Details of the operation remai

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Tusk clash and fake nude images of Italy’s prime minister. Politicians caught in the AI trap
content:
From AI-generated political clashes on Polish television to fake intimate images of Italy’s prime minister, artificial intelligence has turned the internet into an automated factory of disinformation.

Artificial intelligence is an extraordinary tool capable of dramatically accelerating work and unlocking entirely new levels of creativity. The problem begins, however, when innovation starts to outpace regulation and technology turns into a weapon.

The rapid development of AI means that legal frameworks and oversight mechanisms are often unable to keep up with reality, fuelling a constant information war online. Algorithms are now being used to create sophisticated disinformation campaigns and deepfakes targeting some of the most polarising political and social issues.

AI-generated images and audio no longer resemble crude computer graphics — they increasingly look like fragments of real life. Digital technology has advanced to the point where distinguishing between truth and manipulation is becoming nearly impossible for the average viewer.

The end of the entry barrier: from labor
snippet: From AI-generated political clashes on Polish television to fake intimate images of Italy’s prime minister, artificial intelligence has turned the internet into an automated factory of disinformation.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Poles reject stronger presidency, ex-president says, blaming Karol Nawrocki
content:
“The more people observe President Nawrocki in office — and above all his political language — the further they will move away from the idea of a presidential system,” former Polish president Aleksander Kwaśniewski told FocusEurope.pl.

Aleksander Kwaśniewski argues that, paradoxically, Karol Nawrocki himself could become the biggest obstacle to his own constitutional project.

“The idea promoted by President Karol Nawrocki — even if not stated explicitly — amounts to support for a presidential system. I would warn against such a solution,” Kwaśniewski said.

He recalled that during the work of the Constitutional Commission between 1993 and 1997, lawmakers also examined proposals aimed at strengthening the presidential model.

“However, the more people watched Lech Wałęsa’s presidency in practice, the further they moved away from the concept of a strong presidential system,” he explained.

President Karol Nawrocki announced the need for constitutional reform during the celebrations marking Poland’s Constitution Day on 3 May. He also unveiled plans to establi
snippet: “The more people observe President Nawrocki in office — and above all his political language — the further they will move away from the idea of a presidential system,” former Polish president Aleksand
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: ‘Virtual Politics’ Detached From Reality: Sudeten German Congress Divides Czech Politics
content:
According to experts, the controversy has surprised observers in Germany and risks unnecessarily burdening Czech-German relations.

“I do not think Prime Minister Markus Söder and Minister Alexander Dobrindt will enjoy their day in Brno,” Czech Foreign Minister Petr Macinka (Motoristé sobě) told the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung at the beginning of May.

Macinka was referring to the planned visit of Bavarian political leaders to Brno, where they were set to attend the annual congress of the Sudeten German Association – held for the first time on Czech territory. The Sudeten Germans are an ethnic German minority formerly living in the Czech borderlands, many of whom were expelled from Czechoslovakia after the Second World War.

The remarks, echoed by other members of the Czech governing coalition, quickly sparked reactions both domestically and in Germany, Prague’s most important partner within the European Union.

According to German studies expert Vladimír Handl from Charles University’s Institute of International Studies, much of the debate distorted political reality.
snippet: According to experts, the controversy has surprised observers in Germany and risks unnecessarily burdening Czech-German relations.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “This truly is shocking.” Is it permitted to take CSDD exams in Russian?
content:
What do the regulations of the Road Traffic Safety Directorate (CSDD) actually say? Delfi looked into who is allowed to use another language during the exams and under what circumstances.

Claims that all drivers in Latvia can take CSDD exams in Russian are misleading. Latvian citizens and non-citizens may take the exams only in Latvian. Exceptions apply only to foreigners who do not know the state language.

On Tuesday, the 5th of May, members of parliament (MPs) discussed amendments to the Road Traffic Law at a meeting of the Saeima Committee on National Economy, Agrarian, Environmental and Regional Policy. According to information published on the Saeima website, National Alliance (NA) MPs Artūrs Butāns and Jānis Vitenbergs submitted an amendment the law:

“Training and examinations for vehicle drivers, both theoretical and practical, shall take place in the state language. The Road Traffic Safety Directorate has the right to provide the aforementioned driver training and examinations also in the official languages of European Union Member States or the official languages of European Economic Area Member States.”
snippet: What do the regulations of the Road Traffic Safety Directorate (CSDD) actually say? Delfi looked into who is allowed to use another language during the exams and under what circumstances.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: After Accusing the Baltic States of Involvement in the War, Russia Has a Plan
content:
Experts emphasize that such information operations are not isolated or random. They are deliberately employed not only to spread fear and mistrust, but also to undermine Western unity and support for Ukraine.

Russia may have planned a provocation against Lithuania in an effort to create the impression that Lithuania is contributing to Ukraine’s drone attacks against Russia. It is believed that this may have involved a staged incursion by a drone into Lithuanian territory, which could later be used for propaganda purposes and as a pretext to escalate military tensions, particularly in the Kaliningrad region.

Lithuanian officials are calling these allegations disinformation and are warning of a possible escalation. 

The reports also highlight the growing tension in the Baltic region, with drone incidents being reported with increasing frequency in Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia. Politicians are initiating discussions on additional security measures, including the possibility of amending the Constitution to allow NATO forces capable of transporting nuclear weapons to operate within the country’s territory.
snippet: Experts emphasize that such information operations are not isolated or random. They are deliberately employed not only to spread fear and mistrust, but also to undermine Western unity and support for
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Can Hungary remove Orbán’s appointees, like the President, without breaking the rule of law? The EU is unlikely to intervene
content:
If they refuse, Hungary could witness the first major wave of institutional change since the democratic transition. According to legal experts, officials associated with Fidesz could be replaced while still respecting both Hungarian and European rule-of-law principles.

The first truly significant phase of dismantling the Fidesz system may begin in June. It remains unclear exactly how public office holders and heads of institutions appointed in previous terms will be removed, but Magyar has repeatedly stated that his government intends to proceed.

As the Tisza Party secured a constitutional majority on 12 April, the most likely route is the rewriting of the constitution to replace figures associated with Fidesz.

The past few weeks have been devoted to laying the groundwork for this reform. Péter Magyar and the Tisza government have sought in various ways to demonstrate why it is necessary to remove individuals who have become symbols of the Fidesz system over recent years.
snippet: If they refuse, Hungary could witness the first major wave of institutional change since the democratic transition. According to legal experts, officials associated with Fidesz could be replaced while
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: EastFlank Podcast #6 Is Europe ready for conflict?
content:
Welcome to the new episode of the East Flank Podcast! How relevant is the EU defence clause in today’s security environment? While it remains largely absent from public debate across the Eastern Flank, some experts argue that it could prove to be a valuable tool in countering hybrid threats.

In this episode of the East Flank Podcast, journalists from the East Flank editorial team discuss the clause’s potential significance and examine a broader challenge facing the continent: is Europe ready for conflict? As always, the discussion is based on the latest East Flank Report

The post EastFlank Podcast #6 Is Europe ready for conflict? appeared first on Eastflank.
